item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some questions I have after reading this article:

  • If the infection can spread to any and all solid objects, why hasn't it spread past these three areas? It can clearly spread to new areas given that it infected the site.
  • What is "the outline of an arm … grown along the spine"? I'm having trouble visualizing what this could mean.
  • What's up with the city? I'm left wanting more about that. Not essential, but if you ever decide to expand this article I'd recommend going in that direction.

I'd like to reword some things in this article, if that's okay. (Does that count as a minor edit?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't buy the kids asking. If the mutations are so varied and random, why would they recognize a 'normal' one as odd, and not as a different mutation?
The adults know. But the kids have no reason to be surprised by a new body configuration… right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implication that at 2756-B the only difference is how people reacted and that instead of eventually getting on with life, they just rioted/panicked themselves into oblivion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about the general wording of the article feels a little clunky to me, probably that there's a black box every sentance or two. I also dislike the city's inclusion, it seems like it was intended to be a bit ominous but it comes off as rather bland and uncessisary to me.

Still though, I like the concept in general, especially that the Foundation site is still functioning. +1 but I'd really like to see a few minor edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heads-up; you can edit your posts by clicking "Options" and then "Edit". It's a lot more efficient than making an entirely new post and it helps prevent spam build-up in the forums.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E.g. "have murdered".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some grammar errors and odd sentence structure, and it's too similar to existing articles. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is pretty interesting - it's a different approach to an old concept. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you look at the dates, you'll notice that the first town (SCP-2756-A) was discovered around a month after the scp started to spread, so isolation was dealt with by the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did the town manage to isolate itself for multiple years? Are there no roads leading to/from it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keters don't have to be apocalypse-causing, we're-all-going-to-die-for-our-sins type SCPs. Keters only have to be nearly impossible to contain, like SCP-106 or SCP-1155. That would make this SCP Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but it's not actually dangerous in and of itself. Also containment seems to be good. It wouldn't be Safe, because it would still be bad if it got out, I'd have to say Euclid though. Because if this got out it would probably cause mass panic and that may cause loss of life, but once everyone got used to it like in the town, it would be fine.

I could see it being Keter if the hostile growths in SCP-2756-B spread to people, and those started trying to get out into the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to disagree with you on the Keter bit. The virus grows on buildings and infects people, but doesn't kill them. If one of the infected boarded a commercial jet, can you imagine how fast it would spread? Everybody on the plane, and at that airport could be infected. And when that plane lands…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obligitory 610 comparison, but I actually prefer this. 610 is mostly just zombie-body-horror. No dis on 610, it's pretty great, but I like this concept so much better. Rather than gross murderous zombies, the only effect it has on people is the physical one. Mentally they're still intact, and they have to live with it.

My only gripe would be the Keter rating. While it would certainly be a huge information breech if this got out, it's not really that dangerous.

EDIT: I saw someone in the discussion mention this is your first SCP. If that's true, color me slightly impressed. Get's my up vote regardless. I would work on the wording a bit, it's awkward at times. That's not enough for me to down vote though, I would hate to see this go.

item.title: Bravo
item.content:encoded:
I very much like the flow of the article, but I feel that it's a bit dry until the interview log. I upvoted after reading that. There's a couple lower case "i's" in there that you should go through and clean up, but that's more of a nitpick.

I'm also not too sure that Keter is the right object class either, victims are not killed by the mutations, and containment seems to be successful and simple. It wouldn't be Safe because the uncertainty of how it spreads, so maybe Euclid would be the correct class. I'll leave this open to any staff or more experienced writers because I could be completely wrong.

Overall the article puts a nice spin on the typical "omg infectious thing that will likely kill you" cliche, because it doesn't kill you and has the story of how people go on living with it, plus a whole site not being terminated like what would usually happen in a typical Foundation story.
This just seems like a combination of The Flesh That Hates and the one about the town in the middle of the dust bowl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The attempted subversion of "omg scary body horror" is all right, I guess. I feel it could have been done a little more deftly than with a convenient guy in an interview literally telling us that for certain people this is normal now.

This article feels kind of clunky to me in both the concept and the details. Like, if this infects literally everything, living or not, what stops it from spreading to everything in the world? It seems to be localized to these two towns, but no mention of whether or not it's stopped there, or what the implications are of that, is made. There's also odd little things like mentions of formal study of the anomaly's "invasiveness", a term that doesn't really mean anything in the context you're writing in. And how exactly are organs and such grown by inanimate objects "functional"? Like, they do their intended functions? How could that work independent of a living system of some kind?

There might be something workable in all of this, but this just feels like too much of a dissonant reading experience for me to want to keep it around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Calling it a mutation rubs me the wrong way (the mutations I'm familiar with are changes in DNA) and while I like the direction it tried to take, I feel it doesn't go far enough. Down vote.

I would really suggest doing something similar to the interview, but about life in Site-██. A description of badly mutated researchers doing normal Foundation things would be much more interesting (for me at least) than just the account about mutated civilians. Site-██ can be a bit of a paradox; Foundation staff trying to study and fight against the anomalous despite being anomalous themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You say it's an infection, but at no point do you detail how it is spread or how to avoid infection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the concept of a non-lethal disease that horribly disfigures people, yet the infected learn to live with it. Awkward wording in some places, but overall, nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd suggest you move the sentence stating what 2756-B is to the paragraph where you talk about it. I initially downvoted because I couldn't figure out what 2765-B was at first.

+1 otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Site/area designations are the only thing that's OK to censor in the Containment Procedures. If you're working with the SCP, you already know what site it is located at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out reminding me too much of the Flesh That Hates, but I like the direction it went. It horribly mutates people, but not in a harmful way. It just looks gross.

I like the last part of the interview, when he talks about the kids. They just go about their lives like it's been like this forever. Creepstown.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a little bit too flat and obvious for me to upvote, but it's solid. I wouldn't want it gone.

+1

Edit: Erm… I mean 0. Man, where is my head?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like where you went with the interview, but I found the description and to some degree, the item kind of weak. This is a great first attempt, and you should be proud of it, but I found myself disinterested while reading the description, so I novoted.

Still, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I certainly don't dislike it - the idea of everyone having horrific mutations and just trying to live with them is actually kind of sweet, in a weird way. I can't quite put my finger on what it is, but there's just something that's keeping me from happily upvoting this one. You've got my +1, but I was tentative to do it.

Also, you should remove the black boxes on the description. Personnel should be able to read the containment details and know exactly what needs to be done; as it stands now no one will have any idea as to what site anything should be sent to. Overall you want to avoid redaction, expungement, and black boxing anything in the containment procedures, as that's the information any site personnel can access in an emergency. There's no help on attempting to contain something if you can't see how you're supposed to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So Site██ is also full of mutated researchers and stuff?
I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The town is just trying to live as normally as they can with horrible mutations.

It actively foreshadows what could happen should the Foundation ever lose containment of one of their more… esoteric items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The overall effect is very weak, but I do quite like the direction that you took in the interview. It's not quite there for a novote or upvote, but it was a nice little spin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe that would be appropriate, as you are transitioning from one idea to another in a block of text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there supposed to be a paragraph break at the mention of SCP-2756-B?

Should there be? Because I'll fix that if needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nice take on a horrifying mutational disease. It was sounding like 610 for a bit, but thankfully it didn't go down the same path.

Is there supposed to be a paragraph break at the mention of SCP-2756-B?

Edit: changing to novote, I was relived that there wasn't another mediocre article before looking at the details

item.title: First Things First
item.content:encoded:
I would like to thank the guys on chat for reviewing this countless times, and the general community for giving grew help on the forums.

Thanks for helping me pull this through.
